Article.ArticleTitle: Running a risk? Sport supplement toxicity with ephedrine in an amateur marathon runner, with subsequent rhabdomyolysis.

Abstract.AbstractText: A 36-year-old man presented to the emergency department acutely unwell after being found collapsed while running a halfmarathon. He presented with a reduced Glasgow coma score, was tachycardic, agitated, hypoxic and profusely sweating. He had taken a 'supplement' given to him prior to the race by a friend, as he was concerned about not finishing. This contained both caffeine and a large dose of ephedrine (60 mg in total). After initial resuscitation he was intubated, and was transferred to critical care. He subsequently developed rhabdomyolysis, requiring haemofiltration.

AffiliationInfo.Affiliation: Department of Critical Care, City Hospital, Birmingham, UK. rrhidian@doctors.org.uk

Reference.Citation: Rang HP, Dale MM, Ritter JM, et al. Pharmacology. Tottenham Court Rd, London: Elsevier Churchill Livingstone; 2005:176–183

Petróczi A, Naughton DP. Supplement use in sport: is there a potentially dangerous incongruence between rationale and practice? J Occup Med Toxicol 2007;2:4.
